--

Easy to do as well.
Consider if you been curating a command line tool or other program for years and It has grown over time. It has dozens of files, hundreds of functions, and print() statements sprinkled everywhere.
Then one day you feel the need or someone from Germany tells you it sucks because of no such logging… to add session logging, and perhaps some other features to the print. It can be a lot of risk to replace every print in your code. Although with modern tools, it may not be as much of a risk as before. But it is still there.
There’s a cleaner, completely transparent, and old school alternative: monkey‑patching sys.stdout.
And no this isn’t a Python only special. However, the implementation here in the article is specific to only Python. In fact, monkey-patching is also called an intercept. On IBM Mainframes, it is in fact common for 3rd party software vendors to do this type of intercept. It was called a SVC intercept on a Mainframe. Which is the System Supervisor call. This is the syscall of the Mainframe operating systems such as MVS or VSE.
The SVC table was simply an array of pointers in low memory. A typical SVC ntercept looked like:

• Save the original SVC routine address

• Replace it with your own routine

• In your routine, decide whether to either run your code, call the routine, modify stuff, or even suppress the call.
